transcript: In a total eclipse, day turns to night for several minutes. While most people focus their attention on the main event, their surroundings also change. Totality reveals a 360-degree view of sunset and sunrise from every direction at the horizon. This effect is caused by light from the sun in areas outside the path of totality. During totality the sky is dark enough for stars and planets like Jupiter and Venus to appear. If you're very lucky the sky is even dark enough for a passing shooting star to become visible. Temperatures may also drop depending on a couple of factors during the eclipse. The time of year, cloud cover, and how long totality lasts. In past eclipses, the air temperature has been known to drop by as much as 20 degrees Fahrenheit. Eclipse viewers may also get a chance to see a rare phenomenon called shadow bands. They're thin, wavy lines of alternating light and darkness. Their motion is often compared to a snake slithering on the ground, which is why these bands are also sometimes called shadow snakes. They only occur during the seconds before and after totality. Reporting for AccuWeather, I'm meteorologist Jeff Cornish.
